does: This paper systematically surveys methods for evaluating attitudes, opinions, and values (AOVs) embedded in large language models. It reviews recent evaluation approaches across the entire pipeline—from prompting strategies and response elicitation to measurement techniques and result interpretation—identifying how different methodologies can yield divergent findings on the same phenomena. The paper maps the landscape of AOV evaluation work, categorizes existing methods, and highlights inconsistencies across studies that make it difficult to compare results and draw reliable conclusions about what cognitive-behavioral traits LLMs actually express or possess.
connects: This work responds to growing interest in understanding whether and how LLMs capture human-like psychological properties, building on prior research in machine learning interpretability, bias detection, and value alignment. It connects to earlier work on measuring stereotype bias, political leaning, and other demographic-linked behaviors in neural language models, as well as emerging literature on human-AI alignment and whether LLMs should be anthropomorphized. The paper synthesizes disparate evaluation methodologies that have developed in parallel across NLP, psychology, and AI safety communities, acknowledging foundational questions about measurement validity inherited from social science research on attitudes and values.
enables: This work enables more rigorous future evaluation of LLM values and attitudes by establishing clearer evaluation standards and highlighting methodological pitfalls. It points toward interdisciplinary collaboration between computational linguistics, psychology, and sociology to develop validated measurement frameworks. The insights support downstream applications in social science research using LLMs, more principled human-AI alignment efforts, and better understanding of when and why LLMs express particular value positions. It also opens directions for improving model development—via better training, fine-tuning, or prompting—based on deeper understanding of what AOVs models actually encode and how evaluation choices influence interpretations of model behavior.
